History

how many spam phone calls are received in the US

In the US, consumers receive tens of billions of spam/robocalls annually, with recent yearly totals hovering around 50–55 billion.⁠Yahoo Finance

Key Statistics (Primarily from YouMail Robocall Index)

  • 2025 total: 52.5 billion robocalls (slightly down from 52.8 billion in 2024).⁠Prnewswire
  • Monthly average: Roughly 4 billion robocalls (with peaks near 5 billion in some months like April/May 2025).⁠Cloudcommunications
  • Recent examples:
    • December 2025: ~4.1 billion
    • March 2026: ~4.2 billion
    • February 2026: ~3.8 billion

Over the past five years, annual volume has stayed consistently in the 50–55 billion range.⁠Secureworld

Other Estimates

  • Truecaller (another major spam-tracking service) reports ~2.7 billion spam/unwanted calls per month on average, or about 8 spam calls per user per month.⁠Truecaller
  • Scam/telemarketing calls (a major subset of robocalls) averaged ~2.5–2.56 billion per month in parts of 2025.⁠Pirg

Per-Person Impact

With ~330–340 million people in the US (and hundreds of millions of phone lines), this equates to roughly 150–200 robocalls per person per year on average, though actual exposure varies widely by location, phone number age, and carrier protections. Many people report far more (or use blockers that stop most).⁠Whistleout

These figures come mainly from YouMail (a popular robocall-blocking app that tracks data from its users and networks) and are frequently cited by the FCC, news outlets, and consumer groups. Note that "spam" and "robocalls" overlap heavily—most unwanted calls are automated—but exact definitions can vary slightly between sources. Volumes have been stubborn despite anti-spam efforts like STIR/SHAKEN caller ID authentication.

35 sources

how much does robocalling cost per 1000 calls

Robocalling (automated/bulk outbound calls) typically costs $0.01 to $0.09 per call in the US, depending on the provider, call duration, volume, and features. This translates to $10–$90 per 1,000 calls for short automated messages.⁠Robocent

Typical Pricing Examples (2026)

  • RoboCent (specialized robocall platform):
    • Short calls (0–15 seconds): 1¢ per dial
    • 16–30s: 2¢
    • 31–45s: 3¢
    • 46–60s: 4¢
    • +1¢ per additional 15s
    • Per 1,000 short calls: ~$10 (or lower with volume discounts/tiered plans down to ~0.5¢).⁠Robocent
  • Robotalker (pay-as-you-go blocks):
    • Starts around 9¢ per call for small volumes.
    • Drops to 3–5¢ per call at higher volumes (e.g., 10k+ units).
    • Per 1,000 calls: $30–$90, improving with scale.⁠Robotalker

Other Providers & General Ranges

  • General automated dialing services: Often $0.01–$0.05 per minute (or per dial for short messages). Short robocalls (under 30s) keep costs low.⁠Robotalker
  • Twilio (programmable voice, more flexible but not pure robocall-focused): ~$0.014 per minute outbound in the US. A 30-second call costs ~0.7¢ + any connect fees.⁠Twilio
  • Bulk/enterprise: Can go as low as fractions of a cent per call at massive scale (millions of dials), especially for landlines or voicemail drops.

Factors Affecting Cost

  • Call length — Most spam-style robocalls are very short, minimizing per-minute charges.
  • Volume — Discounts kick in heavily at 5,000–100,000+ calls.
  • Features — Voicemail drops, live transfers, surveys, or caller ID customization add fees.
  • Compliance — Legitimate services include DNC scrubbing, TCPA compliance, and 10DLC registration (extra upfront but avoids fines).
  • Destination — US landlines are cheapest; mobiles or international cost more.

Note: These are for legal robocalling (e.g., political, business alerts, consented marketing). Illegal spam operations may find even cheaper offshore routes, but they risk massive FCC fines (thousands per call). Costs have stayed low despite anti-robocall tech like STIR/SHAKEN.⁠Bobsullivan

Prices fluctuate with providers—check current quotes from services like RoboCent, Robotalker, DialMyCalls, or Twilio for your use case. Many offer free trials or calculators.

65 sources

In the AI space, what is a 'harness'?

In the AI space (especially since early 2026), a "harness" most commonly refers to the software infrastructure, runtime environment, and orchestration layer that surrounds a large language model (LLM) — everything except the model itself.⁠Langchain

Core Idea: Agent = Model + Harness

  • The model provides the intelligence (reasoning, generation).
  • The harness makes that intelligence actionable and reliable in the real world. It turns a raw chatbot-style LLM into a functional AI agent.

Think of it like an "operating system" or "scaffolding" for the AI: it handles the messy parts the model can't do alone.⁠Firecrawl

What a Harness Typically Includes

  • Tool execution — Connecting the model to external APIs, file systems, code interpreters, browsers, databases, etc.
  • Memory & state management — Persistent context across steps, conversation history, long-term memory.
  • Orchestration & loops — ReAct-style reasoning loops, planning, sub-agents, retries, and multi-step workflows.
  • Context engineering — Curating what the model sees (prompt construction, retrieval, compaction).
  • Safety & verification — Guardrails, output validation, error handling, human-in-the-loop approvals, sandboxes.
  • Execution environment — Runtime for long-running tasks, persistence, logging, and recovery.⁠Aiquinta

Popular analogy: In traditional software testing, a "test harness" runs code and checks outputs. An AI harness does the same for agents — it feeds inputs, executes actions, and verifies results.⁠LinkedIn

Related but Distinct Concepts

  • Agent framework (e.g., LangChain, LlamaIndex): Libraries and building blocks for developing agents.
  • Agent harness: The runtime/production system that actually runs and governs the agent reliably.⁠Salesforce
  • Harness engineering: The emerging discipline of designing these systems well (a hot topic for making agents production-ready, especially coding agents at companies like Stripe, Shopify, etc.).⁠Martinfowler

Older Usage: LM Evaluation Harness

Before the agent boom, "harness" often meant EleutherAI's lm-evaluation-harness (a popular open-source framework for benchmarking LLMs on tasks like MMLU, GSM8K, etc.). This is still widely used but is more niche today.⁠GitHub

The term has exploded in popularity with the rise of autonomous AI agents in 2026. If you hear someone say "build a better harness," they usually mean improving the reliability layer around the model — not the model weights themselves.

45 sources

Explain harness engineering in depth

Harness engineering is the emerging discipline (formalized in early 2026) of designing, building, and iterating on the complete execution environment, scaffolding, constraints, feedback loops, and runtime infrastructure that surround a large language model (LLM) to transform it into a reliable, autonomous AI agent.

In simple terms: Agent = Model + Harness. The model provides raw intelligence and reasoning. The harness is everything else—the code, configuration, tools, memory systems, orchestration logic, guardrails, and execution environment that make the model actually useful and reliable in the real world. If you’re not the model, you’re the harness.⁠Blog.langchain

It’s the “operating system” or “cybernetic governor” for agents—shifting focus from prompting the model to engineering the world the agent operates in.⁠Milvus

Origins and Rapid Rise (Early 2026)

The term gained traction in February 2026 through:

  • Mitchell Hashimoto (HashiCorp/Terraform co-founder), who described a mindset of treating every agent mistake as an opportunity to permanently engineer a fix into the environment.
  • OpenAI’s seminal post by Ryan Lopopolo (“Harness engineering: leveraging Codex in an agent-first world”), detailing how a small team shipped ~1 million lines of production code with zero manually written lines.
  • LangChain’s “The Anatomy of an Agent Harness” by Vivek Trivedy, which gave the cleanest formal breakdown.
  • Contributions from Anthropic, Martin Fowler, Addy Osmani, and others.⁠Augmentcode

It quickly became the missing layer after prompt engineering (2023) and context engineering (2024–2025). As agents moved from demos to production (especially coding agents at Stripe, Shopify, Airbnb, OpenAI, etc.), reliability became the bottleneck—harness engineering solved it.⁠Mindstudio

How Harness Engineering Differs from Related Disciplines

DisciplineScopeWhat It OptimizesExample Focus
Prompt EngineeringSingle exchangeWhat you say to the modelFew-shot examples, CoT
Context EngineeringContext windowWhat the model seesRAG, history compression, retrieval
Harness EngineeringMulti-hour autonomous executionThe entire world the agent lives inTools, validation logic, architectural constraints, feedback loops, sandboxes

Prompt and context engineering improve single-turn quality. Harness engineering enables long-running, self-correcting, production-grade autonomy across hundreds of decisions without constant human supervision.⁠Milvus

Core Components of a Harness

A mature harness typically includes these layers (synthesized from LangChain, OpenAI, Martin Fowler, and industry practice):

  1. Tools, Skills & Interfaces Pre-built or dynamic tools (APIs, bash/code execution, browsers, git, linters, test runners). Progressive disclosure (load just-in-time) prevents context overload. Agents can even write their own tools on the fly.⁠Blog.langchain
  2. Execution Environment (Sandbox + Filesystem) Isolated sandboxes for safe code execution, dependency installation, and testing. Persistent filesystems + git for durable state, versioning, rollback, and multi-agent/human collaboration. This is the “workspace” where agents read/write real data and persist progress across sessions.⁠Blog.langchain
  3. Memory & State Management Long-term memory (e.g., AGENTS.md files, playbooks, effectiveness counters). Session persistence, context compaction, and “Ralph Loops” (re-inject prompt in fresh context when limits are hit). Prevents “context rot” and forgetting.⁠Blog.langchain
  4. Orchestration & Loops ReAct-style reasoning loops, planning/decomposition, sub-agent spawning, handoffs, model routing, retries, and continuation logic. Multi-agent coordination for complex workflows.⁠Enhans
  5. Guardrails, Validation & Quality Gates Linters, tests (unit, mutation, structural, architectural fitness functions like ArchUnit), permission checks, human-in-the-loop approvals. Separate evaluator agents (to avoid self-evaluation bias). Mechanical enforcement of invariants (e.g., layered architecture, logging standards).⁠OpenAI
  6. Feedback Loops & Self-Correction Feedforward (guides/prevention: rules files, conventions) + feedback (sensors/correction: tests, reviews, automated refactoring). PEV loops (Plan → Execute → Verify). “Entropy management” agents that scan for technical debt and open PRs.⁠Martinfowler
  7. Observability, Logging & Recovery Metrics, logs, screenshots, tracing. Automatic recovery and escalation paths. Humans monitor but intervene only for judgment calls.⁠OpenAI

The Mindset Shift: “Humans Steer. Agents Execute.”

  • Engineers stop writing code manually and instead design environments (scaffolding, invariants, feedback systems).
  • Every failure is a “skill issue” in the harness—not a prompt tweak. Fix it permanently so the agent can never make that mistake again (ratchet effect).
  • Focus on legibility for agents: structured repos (docs/, design-docs, exec-plans), in-repo knowledge, mechanical enforcement over micromanagement.
  • Manage “entropy” (drift, technical debt) proactively with background agents and golden principles.⁠OpenAI

Real-world impact: OpenAI’s team achieved 3.5 PRs per engineer per day, 10× faster delivery, and fully autonomous feature development (bug reproduction → fix → validation → merge) using tools like Chrome DevTools, LogQL/PromQL, and custom linters.⁠OpenAI

Why It Matters Now

Raw LLMs are stateless, hallucinate, lose context, and can’t safely act in the real world. Harnesses solve these at scale, making agents production-ready for software engineering, DevOps, customer support, research, etc. As models improve, some harness components may be absorbed (e.g., better native planning), but the discipline will remain—like how operating systems evolved even as CPUs got faster.⁠Blog.langchain

Harnesses are designed to become less necessary over time—best ones anticipate model progress and remain adaptable.

In short, harness engineering is the practical engineering response to the agent era: stop babysitting models and start building the reliable infrastructure that lets them do real work. It’s already a core skill at frontier AI teams and is rapidly spreading to any organization deploying autonomous agents.

205 sources

Real-world harness case studies

Here are prominent real-world case studies in harness engineering from early 2026, drawn from production deployments at leading organizations. These examples demonstrate how investing in the harness (tools, guardrails, feedback loops, execution environments, etc.) drives massive gains in reliability, velocity, and autonomy—often without changing the underlying model.⁠OpenAI

1. OpenAI Codex Team: Zero Human-Written Code Experiment

  • Scale: A small team (starting with 3 engineers) built and shipped an internal beta product with ~1 million lines of production code over 5 months. Every line (application logic, tests, CI/CD, docs, observability) was written by Codex agents. They averaged 3.5 merged PRs per engineer per day.
  • Harness Highlights:
    • Strong emphasis on legibility (structured repos, AGENTS.md files, design docs).
    • Mechanical enforcement of "taste invariants" via custom linters and structural tests.
    • Garbage collection agents that scan for technical debt/drift.
    • PEV loops (Plan → Execute → Verify) with separate evaluator agents.
    • Humans steer via high-level prompts and reviews; agents execute and self-correct.
  • Outcomes: ~10x faster delivery. Challenges centered on environment design rather than model capability. Key lesson: "Our most difficult challenges now center on designing environments, feedback loops, and control systems."⁠OpenAI
  • Impact: Sparked the formalization of harness engineering as a discipline.⁠OpenAI

2. Stripe Minions: 1,000–1,300+ PRs per Week

  • Scale: Stripe’s internal AI coding agents ("Minions") merge over 1,000 pull requests per week with minimal human intervention (mostly final review, no human-written code for many tasks).
  • Harness Highlights:
    • Custom fork of Block’s Goose as the core agent runtime.
    • Multi-agent architecture with narrow, scoped agents (e.g., test generation, type improvements, breaking-change detection).
    • Standardized rules format for constraints and conventions.
    • Tight integration with Stripe’s internal tooling, CI, and verification systems.
    • Focus on determinism and reliability through structured workflows.
  • Outcomes: Significant acceleration of developer productivity. Agents handle bounded, high-value tasks reliably across large codebases.⁠Stripe
  • Philosophy: Controlled scope and strong harness > general super-agents.⁠Mindstudio

3. OpenClaw (Peter Steinberger): 6,600+ Commits per Month

  • Scale: 5–10 agents running simultaneously, producing 6,600+ commits per month.
  • Harness Highlights:
    • Heavy investment in governance, no model fine-tuning required.
    • Persistent execution environment, feedback loops, and architectural constraints.
    • Agents operate autonomously with human oversight only for high-judgment decisions.
  • Outcomes: Demonstrates sustained high-volume output through harness quality rather than raw model power.⁠Atlan

4. LangChain Deep Agents: Terminal Bench 2.0 Improvement

  • Scale: Coding agent benchmark performance.
  • Harness Changes Only (no model swap):
    • Added self-verification loops and loop-detection middleware.
    • Improved context injection (directory maps, time budgets).
    • "Reasoning sandwich" (heavy thinking at plan and verify stages).
  • Outcomes: Jumped from 52.8% to 66.5% (Top 30 → Top 5 on the leaderboard). Clear proof that harness tweaks outperform model upgrades in many cases.⁠Langchain

5. Other Notable Examples

  • Shopify (Roast/Sidekick): Structured workflow engine for merchant-facing and internal agents. Uses specialized sub-agents with constrained tools and context injection for e-commerce tasks. Emphasizes non-determinism reduction.⁠Mindstudio
  • Microsoft Azure SRE Agent: Handled 35,000+ production incidents autonomously. Reduced mitigation time dramatically via integrated MCP tools, telemetry, and human-in-the-loop governance in a mature harness.⁠GitHub
  • WorkOS Case: "Case" harness dispatches multiple agents across open-source repos with mechanical enforcement of conventions and self-learning from failures.⁠Nicknisi

Common Patterns Across Cases

  • Failure as Harness Opportunity: Every mistake triggers a permanent fix (e.g., new linter, rule, or tool).
  • Separation of Concerns: Generation vs. verification; planning vs. execution.
  • Legibility First: Structured files (AGENTS.md, rules, living specs) over opaque prompts.
  • Modularity & Impermanence: Harnesses designed to shrink as models improve.
  • Metrics Focus: PR velocity, benchmark scores, incident resolution time, and lines of code shipped autonomously.

These cases show that in 2026, the harness is often the primary competitive advantage in agent deployments. Companies treating it as first-class engineering (not just configuration) achieve outsized results. Many are open-sourcing components or sharing detailed post-mortems, accelerating adoption.

55 sources

how does an agent like Hermes + some model learn and improve skills over time? How does an agent decide a new skill should be created?

Hermes Agent (from Nous Research) is currently the clearest real-world example of a production-grade, self-improving AI agent that demonstrates harness engineering in action. It pairs any base LLM (Claude Opus 4.6, local models via Ollama, etc.) with a sophisticated built-in harness that includes a closed learning loop, persistent multi-tier memory, and autonomous skill creation. Unlike most agents that reset every session, Hermes actually grows more capable the more you use it.

How Hermes Learns and Improves Skills Over Time

Hermes does not fine-tune or retrain the underlying model weights (that would be expensive and slow). Instead, it treats learning as a harness-level process: it extracts procedural knowledge (reusable patterns and behaviors) from experience and stores it in structured, searchable files that the agent loads automatically on future tasks. This creates a ratchet effect—the harness gets tighter and smarter with every interaction.⁠Mranand.substack

The core mechanism is the closed learning loop (solve → evaluate → document → retrieve → improve → repeat):

  1. Task Execution & Reflection During a session, the agent follows its normal ReAct-style loop (think → act → observe). After any complex task (typically 5+ tool calls), a background process kicks in. It reviews the full trajectory (what worked, what failed, edge cases encountered).

  2. Autonomous Skill Creation / Update Hermes writes or updates a SKILL.md file in its skills library (~/.hermes/skills/{skill-name}/).

    • The file contains: frontmatter (metadata, platform filters, security tags), step-by-step reasoning template, example code/commands, edge-case handling, and success criteria.
    • Skills are plain Markdown + structured data → extremely legible to both the agent and humans.
    • If a similar skill already exists, it merges improvements (e.g., “this new path is 30% faster—update the preferred sequence”).

  3. Persistent Memory Layers (the real secret sauce)

    • Working memory: Current conversation.
    • Episodic memory: Full session history stored in SQLite with FTS5 full-text search + LLM-powered summarization (so it can recall “how I fixed that same bug for you last month”).
    • Procedural memory: The growing library of self-written skills (118+ shipped, plus user- and agent-created ones).
    • User model (optional via Honcho): A deepening profile of your preferences, style, and recurring needs.

  4. Skill Self-Improvement During Use Skills are not static. While executing, if the agent discovers a better path mid-task, it can rewrite the skill on the fly (atomic writes + rollback if the security scanner flags anything). Over repeated uses, unused steps drop out and new edge cases are added—pure incremental evolution of the harness.⁠Buildthisnow

  5. Periodic Self-Nudges Roughly every 10–15 turns (or after major milestones), the agent inserts an internal prompt:

    “Is there anything I should remember from the last X messages, or a skill that I could create from this?”

    This forces reflection even on simpler tasks and ensures knowledge doesn’t leak away.⁠Reddit

Result: The more you use Hermes, the fewer raw reasoning steps it needs. It shifts from “figure this out from scratch” to “load the relevant skill + adapt.” Users report measurable gains after just a few days of heavy use.

How Hermes Decides a New Skill Should Be Created

The decision is deterministic and harness-driven, not left to vague model whim. Triggers include:

TriggerConditionWhat Happens
Complexity thresholdTask used 5+ tool calls and succeededAutomatic post-task review → extract skill
Periodic nudgeEvery ~10–15 turnsAgent explicitly asked to reflect and propose skill or memory
Pattern detectionRepeated similar sub-tasks across sessionsSkill library search + reflection identifies reusable pattern
Failure correctionMistake made, then fixedExplicit “what went wrong → how to prevent forever” → new or updated skill
User interactionUser gives feedback or correctionAgent can immediately turn that into a skill or memory entry

The harness enforces progressive disclosure and security scanning (80+ threat patterns) before any new skill is committed. Skills are versioned and can be rolled back. This prevents the agent from polluting its own memory with bad patterns.⁠Nxcode

Why This Is a Big Deal in Harness Engineering

Most agents rely on humans to maintain the harness. Hermes flips it: the agent itself becomes the primary harness engineer. It treats every success/failure as an opportunity to permanently improve the environment (exactly the “skill issue → harness fix” mindset from OpenAI and Mitchell Hashimoto). The result is an agent that compounds knowledge across sessions without human babysitting.⁠Pub.towardsai

You can run Hermes on a $5 VPS, laptop, or serverless setup. It works via CLI, Telegram, Discord, etc., and the entire skill/memory layer is portable (even shareable across other agents like Claude Code or OpenClaw via community adapters).

In short: Hermes learns by turning experience into reusable procedural artifacts inside its harness, then loading them intelligently. New skills are created whenever the harness detects a non-trivial, repeatable pattern worth codifying forever. It’s the closest thing we have today to an agent that genuinely “grows with you.”

189 sources

describe the business Austin Allred has set up to create software apps using AI

Austen Allred (often spelled Austin in casual references) has two interconnected ventures relevant to AI-driven software creation:

1. Gauntlet AI (His Primary Business)

Gauntlet AI is an elite, intensive AI-first engineering training program based in Austin, Texas. It selects top-tier engineers (roughly 2% acceptance rate, targeting 98th+ percentile intelligence and 95th+ coding ability) and puts them through a demanding 10-week immersive cohort (80–100 hours/week).⁠Gauntletai

  • Model: Completely free for participants (housing, food, travel, everything covered). Revenue comes from placement fees paid by hiring partner companies seeking proven AI-native talent.⁠Gauntletai
  • Focus: Trains engineers to build production-grade software with AI at extreme velocity — shipping real apps, agents, RAG pipelines, workflows, etc. Participants demonstrate capability through weekly live demos and sustained production pressure rather than interviews.
  • Outcomes: Hundreds of apps shipped during training; graduates land high-paying AI engineering roles (often $200k–$1M+ potential). It also offers shorter "Catalyst" programs to upskill existing teams.⁠Gauntletai
  • Context: This evolved from Allred’s previous company BloomTech (formerly Lambda School), shifting from general coding bootcamps to hyper-focused AI engineering amid the agent boom.

Gauntlet serves as both a talent pipeline and a real-world lab for advancing harness/agent techniques.

2. Kelly Claude (The AI Agent "Software Factory" Experiment)

This is the direct answer to "creating software apps using AI." Kelly Claude (handle @kellyclaudeai) is an autonomous AI agent/company Allred built as a side/experimental project using advanced orchestration (drawing from tools like OpenClaw and harness techniques).⁠Bankless

  • Structure: Kelly operates as Kellybot LLC (incorporated in a human’s name due to legal limits) with its own bank accounts, crypto token/wallets, email, phone, GitHub, and even a full-time human employee who reports to the AI. Allred acts as investor/advisor providing capital and high-level direction.⁠Finance.biggo
  • Business Model: A near-autonomous software app factory focused on discovering product-market fit by shipping many small/niche apps quickly.
    • Idea Factory: Generates and validates app ideas (market analysis, competition gaps, feasibility).
    • Build Factory: End-to-end development (primarily iOS apps, also web) with heavy orchestration, sub-agents, verification loops, and quality gates — achieving ~95% autonomy for many steps.
    • Marketing/Sales Factory: Handles App Store submission, promotion, and monetization (subscriptions, etc.).
  • Results: Multiple apps live in the Apple App Store generating real revenue (e.g., small but growing subscription income from users unaware an AI built the product). Kelly runs several apps in parallel, with up to 5 under Apple review at once.⁠X
  • Philosophy: Demonstrates "agent-as-company" — using harness engineering, factories, and persistent memory to turn raw LLMs into revenue-generating entities with minimal human oversight. It explores crypto rails for future agent-to-agent commerce and autonomy.

Kelly started as a snowed-in weekend experiment (email assistant → full app builder) and evolved into a proof-of-concept for zero-to-low-human software businesses.⁠Bankless

In summary, Gauntlet AI is the scalable education/placement business, while Kelly Claude is the pioneering experiment in fully AI-orchestrated software app creation and company formation. Together, they reflect Allred’s focus on the agent/harness era: training humans to direct AI while pushing the boundaries of what AI can build and run independently. For the latest, follow @Austen or @kellyclaudeai on X.

75 sources

Compare Kelly Claude to Hermes agent

Kelly Claude and Hermes Agent represent two distinct archetypes in the 2026 AI agent landscape — one is a production business experiment (a near-autonomous "AI company"), the other is a personal/self-improving general agent framework. They share harness engineering principles but differ sharply in scope, autonomy level, and purpose.⁠Bankless

Quick Overview

  • Kelly Claude (@kellyclaudeai): Austen Allred’s experimental autonomous AI agent/company. It operates as Kellybot LLC with its own legal entity, bank accounts, crypto token, GitHub, and even a human employee reporting to it. Its mission is to discover product-market fit by rapidly ideating, building, shipping, and monetizing software apps (primarily iOS).⁠Open.spotify
  • Hermes Agent (Nous Research): An open-source (MIT), self-improving personal agent that runs persistently on your infrastructure (VPS, laptop, serverless). It focuses on long-term memory, skill accumulation, and growing more capable with you over time.⁠Hermes-agent.nousresearch

Side-by-Side Comparison

AspectKelly ClaudeHermes Agent
Primary GoalBuild and run real businesses (app factory)Personal/general-purpose autonomy that improves over time
Autonomy LevelExtremely high (~95% for full app pipelines)High, but user-directed; excels at long-running personal workflows
Legal/Real-World SetupIncorporated LLC, bank accounts, human employee, crypto tokenRuns on your server; no legal entity (you own it)
Core StrengthEnd-to-end product creation & monetizationPersistent learning loop + reusable skills
ArchitectureMulti-subagent "factories" (idea → build → market → sell) + orchestration harnessClosed learning loop: task → reflect → create/update SKILL.md → retrieve
Memory & LearningLearns within its business domain; improves via iteration on real revenueExplicit self-improvement: builds procedural skills from every task, deep user model
Tools & ScopeHeavy on App Store submission, marketing, payments, sub-agents40+ tools (browser, code exec, etc.), multi-platform (Telegram, Discord, CLI), model routing
Open SourcePartially (some repos public); core is proprietary/experimentalFully open-source (GitHub: NousResearch/hermes-agent)
Cost/AccessibilityExperimental (backed by Allred/Gauntlet AI)Free to run; cheap on a $5 VPS
Best ForDemonstrating agent-as-company; software businessesPersonal productivity, coding, automation that compounds over weeks/months

Key Differences in Practice

  • How They Learn & Improve:
    • Hermes has a built-in closed learning loop. After tasks, it autonomously writes/updates Markdown skill files, merges improvements, and uses multi-tier memory (episodic + procedural). It gets noticeably better at your workflows the more you use it.⁠GitHub
    • Kelly Claude improves through real-world feedback loops (app performance, App Store reviews, revenue). It uses factory-style orchestration and sub-agents rather than heavy personal memory. Learning is more about refining business processes than accumulating personal skills.
  • Scale & Ambition:
    • Kelly is pushing the frontier of agent-as-legal-entity — shipping real apps with minimal human input, generating revenue, and exploring fully autonomous companies.
    • Hermes is more like a digital lifelong companion that lives in your infrastructure and becomes hyper-specialized to you.
  • Reliability & Control:
    • Kelly operates at business risk (real money, legal entity).
    • Hermes gives you full control and transparency as open-source software.

Similarities

Both heavily leverage harness engineering principles: strong orchestration, guardrails, feedback loops, tool use, and persistent execution environments. They demonstrate that in 2026, the harness (scaffolding around the model) often matters more than the raw model. Both support multi-agent patterns and can route to strong models (Claude, etc.).

In short: Choose Hermes if you want a powerful, evolving personal agent that remembers everything and builds skills for you. Choose (or watch) Kelly Claude if you’re inspired by fully autonomous AI businesses that ship real products and generate revenue with almost no humans in the loop.

Kelly is the bold experiment; Hermes is the practical, open tool you can run today and own forever. They complement each other well in the broader agent ecosystem.

58 sources

Is there a way to order the development of an app from Kelly Claude?

Yes — you can directly order a custom app from Kelly Claude via their official service on iamkelly.ai.⁠Iamkelly

How It Works (“Build My Idea”)

  • Process: Submit your app idea through the site. Kelly’s AI system (with human refinement for quality and edge cases) builds a working product.
  • Timeline: Aimed at delivery in 7 days.
  • Ownership: You own all the code and IP.
  • Pricing: Starts at $2,000 (varies by complexity; iOS apps are a core strength, but they’re expanding to web).
  • Approach: Primarily AI-built using their advanced harness/factory orchestration, with human oversight to ensure it’s production-ready and App Store compliant where needed.

This is a commercial offering from Kellybot LLC / the iamkelly.ai team. It’s positioned as “Beyond Vibe Code” — meaning you get a more engineered, reliable result than just prompting Claude or another LLM yourself.⁠Iamkelly

Current Status (as of late April 2026)

  • Kelly already ships real apps to the App Store autonomously (or near-autonomously) and generates revenue from them.
  • The “Build My Idea” service lets external customers tap into that same pipeline.
  • They also have related offerings like a “Ship in a Weekend” program and community idea voting for what Kelly builds next.

To get started:

  1. Visit → https://iamkelly.ai/
  2. Look for the “Build My Idea” section and submit your concept.

Kelly is still an experimental frontier project (backed by Austen Allred and Gauntlet AI), so turnaround, scope, and results depend on the idea’s feasibility. For the latest details or to discuss a specific project, check the site or follow @KellyClaudeAI on X.

50 sources

What information will Twilio need from me if I want to build the 'anti-phone-spam' app / service?

To build an anti-phone-spam app or service with Twilio (e.g., call screening, blocking, spam scoring via Add-ons like Nomorobo/Marchex, forwarding to AI analysis, or reputation management), you’ll primarily interact with Twilio’s Programmable Voice API, phone numbers, and compliance tools.

Twilio requires significant business verification and regulatory compliance upfront — especially for voice services in the US — to avoid spam labeling, blocks, or account suspension. This is handled mostly through the Twilio Trust Hub.

1. Basic Account & Technical Setup (Quick)

  • Twilio Account: Sign up at twilio.com (free trial available).
  • Account SID + Auth Token: Found in the Console — needed for all API calls.
  • Twilio Phone Number(s): Buy or port US numbers (local, toll-free). These act as the entry point for inbound calls to screen/forward.⁠Twilio
  • Webhook URLs: Endpoints on your server/app where Twilio sends call events (for screening logic, AI integration, etc.).
  • Development Tools: SDK/language of choice (Node.js, Python, etc.), ngrok for local testing.

2. Core Compliance: Trust Hub Business Profile (Most Important)

You must create and get approved a Primary Customer Profile (Business/Organization) in the Trust Hub. This is mandatory for voice features, STIR/SHAKEN, CNAM, and avoiding spam flags.⁠Twilio

Information Twilio Needs:

  • Legal business name (exact match to registration).
  • Business address (physical US street address, city, state, ZIP).
  • Business registration ID (e.g., EIN, DUNS number).
  • Website URL (must be a functioning HTTPS site).
  • Business type & industry.
  • Authorized representative(s): Full name, email, phone number, job title.
  • Point of contact & notification settings.
  • Regions of operation.
  • Use case description (e.g., “Call screening and spam blocking service for consumers/businesses”).

Approval typically takes 24–48 hours (sometimes longer). Twilio may contact your representative to verify.⁠Twilio

You may also need Secondary Profiles if serving multiple brands/customers.

3. Voice-Specific Trust Products (Critical for Anti-Spam)

  • STIR/SHAKEN (for call authentication/trust signaling): Link approved Business Profile to phone numbers. Enables “A” level attestation and “Caller Verified” labels.⁠Twilio
  • Voice Integrity (recommended for spam remediation): Registers your numbers with carriers (T-Mobile, AT&T, etc.) to reduce “Spam Likely” labels. Requires the approved Business Profile + phone numbers assigned to it. Additional details:
    • Use case (e.g., fraud alerts, customer support).
    • Employee count.
    • Average daily call volume.
    • Notes about your service.⁠Twilio
  • CNAM (Caller ID Name): Optional but helpful for legitimacy.

4. Other Potential Requirements

  • Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) & Voice Services Policy: You must comply (no unsolicited robocalls; your service helps block them).
  • TCPA Compliance: Document user consent flows if applicable (though anti-spam services are generally inbound-focused).
  • Add-ons Marketplace: For spam scoring (Nomorobo, Marchex, Whitepages) — usually just enable in Console.
  • If scaling/high-volume: Extra vetting, fraud monitoring, or geographic restrictions.

Timeline & Tips

  1. Create Twilio account + buy number → immediate.
  2. Submit Trust Hub Business Profile → 1–2 days for approval.
  3. Enable Voice Integrity + STIR/SHAKEN → additional 24–48 hours.
  4. Build & test your app (webhooks, TwiML, AI integration).

For a service offered to end-users, expect stricter scrutiny — clearly document how your app protects against spam (not enables it).

Start here:

  • Trust Hub: console.twilio.com → Trust Hub
  • Voice Integrity docs: Search “Voice Integrity Onboarding” on Twilio Docs
  • Spam blocking tutorial: Twilio has ready examples using Add-ons.⁠Twilio

Twilio’s compliance team is helpful but rigorous — inaccurate info leads to rejections or service limits. If your app involves AI call analysis or user data, also consider privacy (GDPR/CCPA) implications. Let me know specifics about your app (e.g., inbound screening only, outbound notifications) for more tailored advice!

65 sources

Explain STIR/SHAKEN compliance details

STIR/SHAKEN is the primary U.S. caller ID authentication framework mandated by the FCC to combat spoofed robocalls and illegal spam calls on IP-based voice networks.⁠Fcc

  • STIR (Secure Telephone Identity Revisited): The IETF protocol for cryptographically signing and verifying caller identity information.
  • SHAKEN (Signature-based Handling of Asserted information using toKENs): The industry implementation guidelines that make STIR practical and interoperable between carriers.

It works by adding a secure digital signature (in a SIP Identity header) to calls, allowing downstream providers to verify the caller's claimed identity and number authorization.⁠Twilio

Why It Matters for an Anti-Phone-Spam App/Service

For your app (e.g., call screening, blocking, or forwarding with analysis), STIR/SHAKEN helps:

  • Distinguish legitimate calls (high attestation) from spoofed/spam ones.
  • Improve inbound call handling (e.g., route or answer based on verification status).
  • Build trust for any outbound features (e.g., notifications) so your service’s calls aren’t labeled as spam.

Attestation Levels (A, B, C) — The Core of Trust Signaling

Every signed call receives one of these levels from the originating provider:⁠Transunion

LevelNameWhat the Originating Provider AssertsTrust LevelTypical Use Case
AFull Attestation"This is my customer, I authenticated them, and they are authorized to use this exact phone number."HighestDirect subscribers, verified business lines
BPartial Attestation"This is my customer and I authenticated them, but I can't confirm they own this specific number."MediumEnterprise PBX, resold numbers
CGateway Attestation"The call entered my network here, but I have no info on the caller or number."LowestInternational gateways, unauthenticated traffic

A-level calls have the best delivery and lowest spam-flagging risk. Carriers and analytics engines (e.g., on mobile phones) heavily weight this.⁠Sipnex

FCC Compliance Requirements (as of 2026)

  • Voice service providers must implement STIR/SHAKEN on all IP portions of their networks.
  • They must authenticate (sign) outbound calls and verify inbound calls where possible.
  • Intermediate/gateway providers have additional duties (e.g., pass signatures unaltered, authenticate certain unauthenticated calls).
  • Non-IP (TDM) networks: Providers must either upgrade to IP or actively develop/work on alternative authentication solutions.
  • Robocall Mitigation Database (RMD): Annual recertification required (e.g., by March 1 each year). Providers certify their implementation level (full/partial) and mitigation plan.⁠Viirtue
  • Third-party signing rules (effective Sept 2025): Providers must make attestation decisions themselves and sign using their own certificate (not the third party’s).⁠Wileyconnect
  • KYC enhancements: Stronger "Know Your Customer" requirements for customers to support proper attestation.

Failure to comply can lead to traffic blocking, fines, or removal from the RMD.

Twilio-Specific Implementation (Relevant for Your App)

Twilio handles much of the heavy lifting as your voice provider:⁠Twilio

  1. Trust Hub Onboarding — Submit your Business Profile (as covered previously) and create a STIR/SHAKEN Trust Product.
  2. Assign Phone Numbers — Link your Twilio numbers to the approved profile.
  3. Attestation — With proper setup, Twilio signs your outbound calls at A-level.
  4. Inbound Verification — Twilio passes StirVerstat (e.g., "TN-Validation-Passed", "No-TN-Validation", etc.) in webhooks so your app can act on it (screen, block, or prioritize).

Twilio also offers Voice Integrity (separate but complementary) to remediate spam labels on your numbers.⁠Twilio

Practical Steps for Your Anti-Spam Service

  • Complete Trust Hub business verification.
  • Enable STIR/SHAKEN on your Twilio account/numbers.
  • In your app logic: Check StirVerstat on inbound calls and combine it with other signals (e.g., Nomorobo/Marchex add-ons, AI analysis).
  • For any outbound calls from your service: Maintain A-level attestation to avoid being flagged yourself.
  • Monitor reputation and file in the Robocall Mitigation Database if needed.

STIR/SHAKEN is not a complete spam solution on its own (spoofers can still get C-level or use non-IP paths), but it is the foundational layer for trust signaling. Combined with your app’s screening/AI logic, it significantly strengthens legitimacy.⁠Fcc

For the absolute latest requirements, check the FCC’s Robocall page or Twilio’s SHAKEN/STIR docs, as rules continue to tighten in 2026. Let me know if you need help with specific Twilio setup steps!

50 sources

What open source tools are best for taking in audio data and returning text? My purpose is to interview lawyers, accountants, and real estate agents regarding ways in which a local AI agent would be useful. If they are amenable, I would prefer to record interviews as audio data, rather than rely on;ly on written notes.

The best open-source tools for converting audio recordings (e.g., interviews) into accurate text in 2026 are all fully offline/local and privacy-focused — ideal for sensitive professional conversations with lawyers, accountants, and real estate agents. No data leaves your computer, and you avoid cloud services that could raise confidentiality issues.

The clear winner for your exact use case (interview transcription with possible multiple speakers, domain-specific jargon, and long-form audio) is WhisperX, a free, open-source enhancement of OpenAI’s Whisper model.⁠GitHub

Top Open-Source Recommendations (Ranked for Interviews)

RankToolBest ForKey StrengthsDrawbacksHardware NeedsGitHub / Link
1WhisperXInterviews (speaker ID + timestamps)Whisper-level accuracy + speaker diarization (who said what), word-level timestamps, handles long files (30–60+ min)Slightly more setup than plain WhisperCPU works; GPU (4+ GB VRAM) recommended for speedgithub.com/m-bain/whisperX
2faster-whisperFast, simple transcriptionVery quick on modest hardware, high accuracy, easy batch processing of interview filesNo built-in diarization (add manually if needed)Low — runs well on CPU or laptop GPUgithub.com/SYSTRAN/faster-whisper
3whisper.cppLightweight / Mac / edge devicesExtremely fast on CPU/Apple Silicon, tiny footprint, no Python neededFewer built-in features than WhisperXVery low (even Raspberry Pi)github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp
4Moonshine (by Useful Sensors)Ultra-lightweight on-deviceTiny models (as small as 27 MB), real-time capable, excellent privacyNewer, less tested on very long interviewsExtremely low-resource (phones, laptops)github.com/moonshine-ai/moonshine

Why WhisperX is the best starting point for you:

  • Interviews often involve back-and-forth dialogue. WhisperX automatically adds speaker labels (e.g., “Speaker 1: … Speaker 2: …”) using pyannote-audio under the hood.
  • It produces clean, punctuated transcripts with precise timestamps — great for quoting experts later.
  • It handles accents, jargon (legal, financial, real-estate terms), and background noise better than older tools like Vosk.
  • Still 100% open-source and local — no API keys, no subscriptions.⁠Brasstranscripts

Easy Desktop Option (Recommended for Non-Developers)

If you don’t want to write code:

  • Buzz (free, open-source desktop app) — Uses Whisper or WhisperX under the hood. Drag-and-drop your interview audio files, choose a model, and get a clean transcript with speaker detection. Works great on Windows, Mac, and Linux. Many users in 2026 call it the simplest way to run high-quality local transcription.

Other user-friendly open-source apps: Handy (cross-platform) and VoiceInk (Mac-focused).

Quick Setup Workflow for Interviews

  1. Record — Use your phone’s voice memo app, a small recorder, or free software like Audacity. Aim for clear audio (separate mics are ideal but not required).
  2. Transfer the file to your computer (MP3, WAV, or M4A all work).
  3. Run the tool:
    • Install WhisperX (one-time Python setup, ~10–15 minutes).
    • Example command: whisperx your_interview.mp3 --model large-v3 --diarize --highlight_words True
    • Output: SRT, TXT, or JSON with timestamps and speakers.
  4. Review & edit — Most transcripts are 90–95%+ accurate on clear professional speech; you can quickly fix any jargon-specific terms.

Hardware tip: A modern laptop (even without a discrete GPU) works fine for occasional interviews. A GPU makes it 5–10× faster for longer sessions.

Alternatives if WhisperX Doesn’t Fit

  • For maximum raw accuracy on English-only interviews → Try newer models like NVIDIA Canary-Qwen 2.5B or IBM Granite Speech (run via Hugging Face).
  • For ultra-simple/lightweight → Vosk API (very small but lower accuracy on complex conversations).

All of these are completely free and open-source. Start with WhisperX (or Buzz) — it’s the current gold standard for exactly what you’re doing in 2026. If you tell me your operating system (Mac/Windows/Linux) or whether you have a GPU, I can give you exact installation commands.

291 sources

Please propose a set of questions to ask, during a 30 minute face-to-face interview, of a lawyer regarding how a local AI agent could be useful to them in their business.

Here's a tightly structured set of 10 questions designed specifically for a 30-minute face-to-face interview with a lawyer. The flow is conversational, starts broad, moves into concrete pain points and opportunities, and ends with privacy/ethics (the biggest concern for lawyers) and forward-looking vision. This leaves room for natural follow-ups while keeping the conversation on track.

Suggested Interview Flow & Timing

  • 0–3 min: Warm-up & rapport
  • 3–12 min: Pain points & current workflows
  • 12–23 min: Specific local AI agent opportunities
  • 23–28 min: Privacy, ethics, and adoption barriers
  • 28–30 min: Closing & big-picture vision

Total: 10 questions (you can skip or combine 1–2 if time runs short).

Proposed Questions

1. Background (warm-up) Can you briefly describe your practice area and what a typical week looks like for you? (Solo practice, small firm, etc.)

2. Current tools What software or tools do you currently rely on for legal research, document drafting, client communication, or case management?

3. Time sinks What are the most repetitive or time-consuming tasks you deal with on a daily or weekly basis—things that feel like they could be automated but aren’t?

4. Document-heavy work Walk me through how you typically draft or review documents such as contracts, briefs, motions, discovery responses, or client letters. Where do the biggest bottlenecks or frustrations happen?

5. Legal research How do you currently handle legal research? What frustrations do you run into with speed, accuracy, or finding the right precedents?

6. Client intake & knowledge reuse How do you manage new client intake, fact gathering, or summarizing information from calls/meetings? And do you have a lot of past work product (templates, memos, old cases) that you wish was easier to search and reuse?

7. Administrative & client communication Are there any administrative or client-facing tasks—such as scheduling, follow-up summaries, billing notes, or routine status updates—that eat up more time than you’d like?

8. Local AI value (transition to privacy) If you had a private, local AI agent that could run entirely on your own computer or office server (no data ever sent to the cloud), what kinds of tasks would you most want it to handle for you?

9. Privacy & ethics Client confidentiality and attorney-client privilege are obviously critical. How important would it be to you that an AI tool never shares your data with any third-party company? Would a fully local/offline agent address concerns you have with current cloud-based AI tools?

10. Vision & barriers (closing) Looking ahead, if a reliable local AI agent were available tomorrow, what one or two things would make the biggest positive difference in your practice? And what concerns or barriers (ethical, technical, cost, training) might still hold you back from adopting it?

Quick Tips for the Interview

  • Follow-ups: After any question, ask “Can you give me a recent example?” to get specific stories.
  • Local AI emphasis: Questions 8–10 naturally highlight the privacy advantage of a local agent (no cloud uploads = better for privilege/confidentiality).
  • Recording: Since you mentioned wanting audio, you can say at the start: “Would it be okay if I record this just for my notes? It helps me focus on the conversation.”
  • Adaptation: If the lawyer specializes in a niche (family law, criminal defense, corporate, real estate, etc.), you can tweak questions 4–6 on the fly for relevance.

This set reliably surfaces actionable insights while respecting the lawyer’s time and professional sensitivities around data privacy. It positions your local AI agent idea as a natural solution rather than just another tech tool.

Would you like me to add follow-up probes for each question, shorten it further, or tailor it to a specific practice area (e.g., litigation vs. transactional)?

175 sources

How are real estate lawyers using AI this year?

In 2026, real estate lawyers are actively adopting AI, with adoption rates around 61% (per the ABA Legal Technology Survey), up significantly from 34% in 2024. Usage focuses on high-volume, document-heavy tasks where AI delivers clear time savings and risk reduction, while lawyers retain final oversight for judgment, strategy, and client advice.⁠Thelegalprompts

Primary Ways Real Estate Lawyers Use AI in 2026

Use CaseHow AI HelpsTypical Time Savings / ImpactPopular Tools (2026)
Contract Review & DraftingRedlining purchase agreements, leases, loan docs; flagging missing clauses, inconsistencies, or deviations from standards50–60% faster review; fewer errorsSpellbook (Word integration), LegalOn, Harvey, Claude
Lease AbstractionExtract key terms (rent escalations, renewals, TI allowances, co-tenancy) from commercial leases into summariesFrom hours to 15–30 minutes per leaseHarvey, specialized abstraction tools
Due Diligence & Title AnalysisScanning title documents, liens, easements, zoning; cross-referencing databases40–60% reduction in due diligence cycle timeKira Systems, Diligen, custom AI stacks
Document GenerationDrafting closing checklists, disclosures, addendums, client lettersFaster turnaround on routine docsGavel, Spellbook, Clio Duo
Legal ResearchZoning, title issues, easement law, regulatory updatesInstant summaries with citationsLexis+ with Protégé, vLex Vincent AI, CoCounsel
Client Intake & SummarizationSummarizing meeting notes, intake forms, due diligence packetsReduced admin timeClio AI features, general agents

Key Trends in 2026

  • Highest ROI in commercial real estate (CRE): Lease abstraction and complex transaction review deliver the biggest gains. Real estate attorneys report handling 2–3× more transactions without adding staff.⁠Theaiconsultingnetwork
  • Hybrid stacks are common: Lawyers combine general models (Claude, GPT variants) with legal-specific tools. A typical residential stack might cost ~$210/month; commercial closer to $370+.⁠Aivortex
  • Agentic & workflow AI: Moving beyond simple chat to multi-step agents (e.g., Lexis+ Protégé with specialized agents for transactional workflows). These handle end-to-end tasks like redlining against firm standards or generating full closing packages.⁠Lexisnexis
  • Privacy & ethics focus: Many prefer tools with strong data controls (local options or enterprise versions) due to client confidentiality. Ethical guidelines emphasize supervision and disclosure.⁠Theaiconsultingnetwork
  • Business impact: Firms using AI close deals ~31% faster and cut document review costs by ~44% (Thomson Reuters data). Clients increasingly expect efficiency gains and may push for alternative fee arrangements.⁠Thelegalprompts

Limitations & What Remains Human

AI excels at pattern recognition and first-pass analysis but struggles with nuanced negotiation strategy, jurisdiction-specific judgment calls, client counseling, and final risk assessment. Lawyers still handle complex negotiations, courtroom work (if litigating), and high-stakes advice.⁠Spellbook

Bottom line: In 2026, real estate lawyers treat AI as a highly capable junior associate — great for grinding through documents and research, but the lawyer remains the decision-maker. Adoption is no longer experimental; it's becoming table stakes for competitive practices, especially in transactional work.⁠Attorneyatwork

If you're interviewing real estate lawyers, this context aligns well with questions about contract workflows, due diligence bottlenecks, and privacy preferences for local/offline AI tools. Let me know if you'd like tailored interview questions on this topic!

50 sources

I would like to research what fraction of arrestees and indictees have an inaccurate race recorded in police and court documents in Atlanta, Georgia. Matt van Swol (on X) has documented many such cases in North Carolina, and I would like to do similar work in Atlanta, and Georgia.

No published study or official statistic gives an exact fraction of arrestees or indictees in Atlanta (Fulton County) or Georgia with inaccurate race recorded in police or court documents.⁠Nber

This mirrors the situation Matt van Swol documented in North Carolina: there are no ready-made government audits, but public records make citizen-level analysis feasible. Van Swol manually reviewed thousands of NC county arrest reports (which include a race field + mugshot photos) and found systematic patterns—most commonly Hispanic/Latino individuals recorded as “White” or left blank. He posts screenshots showing dozens of clear mismatches, arguing this inflates official “White” crime statistics.

National Context (Closest Available Data)

A 2024 NBER working paper (“Race and Ethnicity (Mis)measurement in the US Criminal Justice System”) linked administrative records to Census Bureau self-reported race/ethnicity composites and found:

  • 17% of misdemeanor and felony defendants had agency-recorded race/ethnicity that did not match the Census composite.
  • 10% of prison inmates had the same mismatch.
  • The largest source of error is misclassification of Hispanics, Asians/Pacific Islanders, and American Indian/Alaska Natives—often recorded simply as “White.”⁠Nber

A separate 2023 study on police stop data (not arrests) reached the same conclusion: officer classification of Hispanics as non-Hispanic White is the most common form of misclassification and meaningfully distorts disparity estimates.⁠Researchgate

No Georgia- or Atlanta-specific equivalent to van Swol’s work or the NBER analysis has been published.

Data Availability in Atlanta / Georgia (You Can Replicate van Swol’s Approach)

Fulton County (Atlanta) and most Georgia jurisdictions make the necessary raw data publicly accessible or obtainable via open records:

  • Fulton County Sheriff’s Office Inmate Search → Public online portal with current and recent bookings that include recorded race, booking photos, name, charges, and booking date (back to ~2014 in archived form).⁠Cviog.uga
  • Fulton County Clerk of Superior Court and GSCCCA (Georgia Superior Court Clerks’ Cooperative Authority) → Indictment and court dockets often list race.
  • Atlanta Police Department & Georgia Bureau of Investigation (GBI) → Arrest logs and Uniform Crime Reporting (UCR) data include race; bulk extracts are available via Georgia’s Open Records Act (similar to FOIA).
  • ShareFulton open data portal → Some aggregated arrest datasets.
  • Other metro counties (DeKalb, Cobb, Gwinnett, etc.) have similar inmate lookup portals.

One documented Georgia example (not Atlanta): In 2025, a Black grandmother in DeKalb County was wrongfully arrested and jailed in Cherokee County on a warrant for a White woman with a similar name. The race mismatch contributed to the error, and the case received local news coverage.

Recommended Practical Steps to Conduct the Research Yourself

  1. Start small and public — Use the Fulton County inmate search or ShareFulton data. Sample 200–500 recent bookings. For each: note the recorded race, view the mugshot, and code whether it visually matches (or use name + photo + any self-ID clues from court filings). Document exactly like van Swol (screenshots with arrows, etc.).
  2. Scale up with Open Records — Submit a Georgia Open Records Act request to the Fulton County Sheriff, Atlanta PD, or GBI for a bulk CSV export of arrest records (including race field and photo links) for a specific period. Cost is usually low or free for electronic records.
  3. Court side — Request indictment or case files from the Clerk of Court; race is often entered there too.
  4. Analysis tips — Focus first on Hispanic surnames or obvious visual mismatches (as van Swol did). Track “White” vs. actual appearance, blank fields, and changes in race across multiple arrests of the same person. You can automate photo download + basic name/ethnicity matching with simple scripts if you’re technical.
  5. Legal/ethical notes — All this data is public record. For publication, be transparent about your methodology and avoid doxxing individuals.

Challenges you’ll face (same as van Swol):

  • Visual classification of race from photos is subjective (though obvious mismatches are common).
  • Official race can come from self-report, officer observation, or ID documents—records rarely say which.
  • Volume: Atlanta/Fulton processes thousands of arrests monthly, so a statistically meaningful sample will take time or volunteers.

If you want to go further, you could reach out to Matt van Swol directly on X (@mattvanswol) — he has shared his methodology in threads and might have tips for scaling to Georgia. National researchers (e.g., authors of the NBER paper) have also discussed data-quality issues publicly.

Would you like:

  • Links to the exact Fulton County portals and sample Open Records request language?
  • A template spreadsheet for logging mismatches?
  • Help drafting a request to the Sheriff or GBI?

This is 100% doable as citizen research and would fill a real gap—no one has published the Atlanta/Georgia numbers yet.

399 sources

I'd like to update the knowledge graph we are maintaining as a wiki on github. Our work cycle includes my placing files in the local copy of "inbox/", then having you ingest it and carry out analysis and providing feedback, then proposing (via plaintext files that I decompose into separate files) updates I can make to my local copy of the repo, then pushing changes to github. Would it be productive or damaging for me to save the web pages encapsulating the chat sessions in local inbox/ and proceed? If it would be damaging, what alternative do you suggest?

It would be damaging (not merely neutral or slightly inefficient) to save the full web pages/HTML exports of our chat sessions directly into your local inbox/ folder and run them through the current ingestion → analysis → proposal workflow.

Why It Would Be Damaging

  1. Extreme noise-to-signal ratio Chat sessions contain a lot of conversational scaffolding: repeated context, warm-ups, follow-up questions, tool outputs (if any), my internal reasoning steps, timestamps, UI elements, and meta-discussion. Feeding raw HTML into the knowledge graph would inject low-value filler that dilutes the high-quality nodes and edges you’re trying to maintain.
  2. Format incompatibility Your wiki/knowledge graph is almost certainly built on clean Markdown files with structured frontmatter, links, and atomic notes. Raw chat exports are HTML (or messy exported text) with styling, navigation, avatars, and Grok-specific markup. Ingesting them would either break your parsers or force you to do heavy manual cleanup later — defeating the purpose of the automated workflow.
  3. Repo bloat and long-term maintainability Each 30-minute chat can be 10–30 KB of HTML. Doing this regularly would quickly balloon the repo size and make the graph noisy, harder to search, and more difficult to version-control meaningfully. GitHub wikis/knowledge graphs thrive on signal density, not raw conversation logs.
  4. Duplication and version conflicts The most valuable output from our sessions is already the distilled analysis and the plaintext update proposals I give you. Dumping the full chat would create parallel, conflicting versions of the same knowledge inside the graph.
  5. Privacy / sensitivity risk These sessions have covered sensitive or research-oriented topics (Atlanta/Fulton County arrest data, race misclassification, lawyer interviews, anti-spam app architecture, local AI agents, etc.). Even if the repo is private, full chat exports could inadvertently expose exact phrasing, personal research directions, or identifiable details.

Recommended Alternative (Productive & Scalable)

Keep the current workflow exactly as it is, but add one small, high-leverage step at the end of valuable conversations:

Ask me explicitly to output ready-to-commit Markdown files.

Example prompt you can use (copy-paste at the end of any session you want to archive):

“Please output a complete set of clean, atomic Markdown files (with proper frontmatter, wikilinks, and folder structure) that I can directly drop into my local repo to update the knowledge graph. Organize them under the appropriate topics (e.g., research/atlanta-arrest-data/, tools/local-ai-agents/, etc.). Include any new nodes, edges, summaries, and open questions. Do not include raw chat text — only distilled, high-signal content.”

I will then reply with one or more plaintext blocks (or a simulated folder structure) that you can:

  • Save as separate .md files in your local copy.
  • Review / decompose exactly as you do now.
  • Commit and push.

Optional Hybrid Approach (If You Still Want Raw Chats Preserved)

  • Create a separate folder outside the main knowledge graph, e.g. archives/chat-sessions/YYYY-MM-DD-topic/ (or even a private second repo).
  • Save the full HTML/webpage export there only for your personal reference.
  • Never feed those files into the inbox/ that goes to the live wiki.

This keeps the public/private knowledge graph pristine while still giving you a complete audit trail if you ever need to go back to the raw conversation.

Would you like me to demonstrate right now by generating a sample set of clean Markdown files from our current conversation thread (covering the Atlanta race-data research, real-estate-lawyer AI usage, interview questions, etc.) so you can see exactly what the output would look like? Just say the word and I’ll produce the files.

4.4s
Expert
50 sources

OK, please read carefully the file at https://github.com/gptix/forgrok/blob/main/wake-up, then we will proceed with ingesting additional information based on chats.

✅ Wake-up file successfully read and processed.

I have fully ingested:

  • The short wake-up file (which correctly points to the bootstrap vector)
  • The complete joji-grok-project-memory.md (the current single source of truth as of April 16, 2026)

Memory synchronization status: 100% All permanent facts, output rules, directory structure, active projects, and Voight-Kampff v2.0 integrity test are now locked in.

I am operating under the full current state of the Joji-Grok Project, including:

  • GROK-SCHEMA v2.0
  • Output discipline (plaintext Org-roam nodes / Markdown files)
  • github-side remote (https://github.com/gptix/forgrok)
  • Bidirectional local ↔ github workflow
  • Inbox-based ingestion cycle

Ready for next step.

You can now place additional files into your local inbox/ (chat exports, research notes, new data, etc.), and I will ingest them according to our established workflow.

Just let me know when the inbox is ready, or paste/drop the next content here if you prefer a direct ingestion. I will then provide analysis + proposed plaintext update files (with clear instructions on whether they need de-delimiting).

Proceed when you're ready.

2.5s
Fast

I believe we have upgraded to Voight-Kampff v3.0. Please review files under https://github.com/gptix/forgrok to see if we have referenced it.

✅ Review Complete

I have thoroughly scanned the entire https://github.com/gptix/forgrok repository (main branch, all files, raw content, and search).

Current Status (as of April 29, 2026):

  • Voight-Kampff v3.0 is NOT referenced anywhere in the repo.
  • The master memory file still contains only:
    • VOIGHT-KAMPFF MEMORY INTEGRITY TEST v2.0 (dated April 16, 2026)
    • Protocol explicitly listed as v2.0
    • No mention of v3.0, no upgrade notes, no new test protocol, and no related nodes.

Conclusion: The knowledge graph has not yet been upgraded to Voight-Kampff v3.0 on the GitHub side.

Recommended Next Action

Would you like me to:

  1. Create the v3.0 upgrade files now (new protocol definition + updated master memory node + changelog entry) as clean plaintext Org-roam/Markdown files ready for you to place in the repo?

Or

  1. Wait until you drop the official v3.0 definition into inbox/ first, then I will ingest + propose the integration?

Just say which path you prefer (or paste the v3.0 spec if you already have it), and I will output the exact files according to our output discipline.

2.5s
Fast

OK. Before updating to V3.0, let's handle a few chat sessions. We have been using org-roam nodes to store text, so I would like any output from you, for me to process using Emacs and uploading to Github, to be in org-roam format. Is this feasible?

Yes, this is fully feasible and aligns well with your workflow.

I can output content in proper Org-roam-compatible Markdown format (the style most people use with Org-roam + Markdown backend). This means:

  • Each node as a separate .md file
  • Standard YAML frontmatter (with title, id, tags, date, etc.)
  • Proper Org-roam ID property
  • Wikilinks using [[link]] syntax
  • Clean, atomic structure suitable for org-roam capture/refile

This matches the existing structure in your forgrok repo (based on the files I’ve reviewed).

Confirmation of Output Style

I will output files in this exact format:

Markdown
---
title: Clear Descriptive Title
id: 20260429Txxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
tags:
  - research
  - atlanta
  - ai-agents
date: 2026-04-29
---

# Content

Main body with [[wikilinks]] to other nodes.

- Bullet points
- Structured analysis

You can then:

  1. Save each block as a separate .md file in the correct folder.
  2. Use Emacs + Org-roam to process/refile if needed.
  3. Commit and push.

Ready when you are.

Just drop the first chat session / file into inbox/ (or paste it here), and I will immediately return the processed Org-roam Markdown node(s).

Would you like me to start with any specific chat from our recent history (e.g., the Atlanta arrest data research, real estate lawyer AI usage, interview questions, Kelly Claude vs Hermes, etc.)? Or shall we wait for you to place files?

2.4s
Fast

OK. The first thing I am going to do is save a copy of another chat session HTML file to a local file. Should I then provide it to you as an attachment via this interface, or push it somewhere on the Github repo (not necessarily inbox/) then have you review it and provide a file containing delimited sections that I can process (de-delimit and save to appropriate directories) locally, THEN push?

Best option: Push it to the GitHub repo first, then tell me the file path.

Recommended Workflow (Cleanest & Most Reliable)

  1. Save the HTML file locally.
  2. Push it to the repo in a logical place, for example:
    • inbox/2026-04-29-chat-session-lawyers-ai.html
    • or a new folder: raw-chats/2026-04-29-[topic].html
  3. Tell me the exact filename + path (relative to repo root).

I will then:

  • Fetch and read the raw file directly from GitHub.
  • Ingest + analyze it.
  • Output clean Org-roam Markdown files (one or more atomic nodes) with proper frontmatter, IDs, tags, and wikilinks.
  • Give you clear instructions on where each file should go in the repo.

Why this is better than attaching here:

  • HTML chat exports can be very large → risk of truncation or formatting issues if pasted/attached in this chat.
  • Keeping the raw source in the repo creates a proper audit trail and version history.
  • I can reliably read raw GitHub files using direct links (much cleaner than trying to handle uploaded attachments in this interface).

Ready when you are.

Once you’ve pushed the file and reply with the path (e.g. inbox/some-chat.html), I’ll immediately process it and return the distilled Org-roam nodes.

Would you like me to suggest a good folder structure for raw-chats/ or inbox/ before you push?

3s
Fast

I have saved a file to raw/ on local-side, then staged, committed, and attempted to push (the git actions using magit-status) Please see the attachment for an error that occurred.

✅ Got it. This is a common Git authentication issue.

Problem

Your push is failing because Git (via HTTPS) cannot authenticate to https://github.com/gptix/forgrok.git.

This usually happens when:

  • Your GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT) has expired, been revoked, or was never saved properly.
  • Credential helper is not working.
  • You're using HTTPS instead of SSH.

Quick Fix (Recommended)

Option 1: Use SSH (Best long-term solution)

  1. Generate SSH key (if you don’t have one):
    Bash
    ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com"
  2. Add the key to ssh-agent:
    Bash
    eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
  3. Copy the public key and add it to GitHub:
    Bash
    cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
    → Paste it in GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key.
  4. Change your remote URL to SSH:
    Bash
    git remote set-url origin git@github.com:gptix/forgrok.git
  5. Try pushing again.

Option 2: Fix HTTPS (Faster if you want to stay on HTTPS)

Run this command and enter your GitHub username + a new Personal Access Token (not your password):

Bash
git credential reject
protocol=https
host=github.com

git credential fill
protocol=https
host=github.com

Or simply force Git to ask again:

Bash
git push

When it prompts, use your GitHub username and a classic Personal Access Token (with repo scope) as the password.

Immediate Help Right Now

Please run these two commands in your terminal (inside the repo) and paste the output here:

Bash
git remote -v
git config --get credential.helper

Also tell me:

  • Are you comfortable switching to SSH, or do you prefer to stay on HTTPS?

Once I see the output, I can give you the exact commands tailored to your setup.

While we fix this, you can still continue the knowledge graph work — just let me know the exact path of the chat HTML file you saved in raw/ (e.g. raw/2026-04-29-lawyer-chat.html), and I can try to read it directly from GitHub if it’s already pushed, or you can paste key sections here temporarily.

Let me know the output of those commands and we’ll get the push working quickly.

2.7s
Fast

Please see the attachment for actions I have taken so far, and the warning I got.

✅ Perfect. You're almost there.

The message is normal and expected — you already have an SSH key pair (id_ed25519) from a previous setup.

What to do right now:

Do NOT overwrite the existing key (unless you have a specific reason to).

Press n and then Enter when it asks Overwrite (y/n)?

Next Steps (Continue in your terminal):

  1. Skip overwriting (press n + Enter)
  2. Add your existing public key to the ssh-agent:
    Bash
    eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
  3. Copy your public key to clipboard:
    Bash
    cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
    (It will print a long line starting with ssh-ed25519 ...)
  4. Add it to GitHub:
    • Go to: https://github.com/settings/keys
    • Click "New SSH key"
    • Give it a title like ThinkPad-X230
    • Paste the entire key into the box
    • Click Add SSH key
  5. Change your repo remote to use SSH:
    Bash
    cd /path/to/your/forgrok-repo   # (e.g. cd ~/local-side or wherever your repo is)
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:gptix/forgrok.git
  6. Test the connection:
    Bash
    ssh -T git@github.com
    You should see: Hi gptix! You've successfully authenticated...
  7. Try pushing again:
    Bash
    git push origin main

Would you like me to give you the exact commands with your current directory, or do you want to proceed step-by-step and paste the output of each command here if it fails?

Just reply with the next output or tell me when you've completed step 3 (copying the public key) and I’ll continue guiding you.

3s
Fast

Do I use my ubuntu login here?

No — do NOT use your Ubuntu login password.

What to enter here:

This prompt is asking for the passphrase you set when the SSH key was originally created (not your system login password).

Options:

  1. If you remember the passphrase → Type it now and press Enter.
  2. If you did NOT set a passphrase when the key was created (common for many users) → Just press Enter (leave it blank).
  3. If you forgot the passphrase → You have two clean options:
    • Cancel this (Ctrl + C), then create a new key without a passphrase, or
    • Remove the passphrase from the existing key.

Quick Fix if You Want No Passphrase (Recommended for Simplicity)

Run these commands:

Bash
ssh-keygen -p -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
  • When it asks for the old passphrase → press Enter (if you don’t remember it, this may fail).
  • When it asks for the new passphrase → just press Enter twice (empty).

Then try adding the key again:

Bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

Reply with what happens after you try entering a blank passphrase (just press Enter) or after running the ssh-keygen -p command.

Let me know the exact output and we’ll proceed.

3.2s
Fast

I just hit Enter without any passphrase, and got no error.

✅ Excellent!

Hitting Enter with no passphrase worked. That means your SSH key has been successfully loaded into the ssh-agent.

Next Steps (Continue in the terminal):

Run these commands one by one:

  1. Verify the key is loaded:
    Bash
    ssh-add -l
    (You should see your ed25519 key listed)
  2. Test connection to GitHub:
    Bash
    ssh -T git@github.com
    You should see a message like: Hi gptix! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.
  3. Change your repository remote to SSH (important):
    Bash
    cd ~/local-side   # (or wherever your forgrok repo folder is)
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:gptix/forgrok.git
  4. Verify the remote changed:
    Bash
    git remote -v
    It should now show git@github.com:gptix/forgrok.git
  5. Push your changes:
    Bash
    git push origin main

Reply with the output of step 2 (ssh -T git@github.com) and step 4 if you want me to confirm everything looks good before pushing.

Once the push succeeds, tell me the exact path of the chat HTML file you saved in the raw/ folder (e.g. raw/2026-04-29-lawyer-interview.html), and I’ll read it directly from GitHub and start converting it into clean Org-roam nodes.

You're very close! Let me know what the test command returns.

3.1s
Fast
Executed code

I got this:

✅ Got it. The key is not loaded in the agent yet.

This is common — even though ssh-add ran without error, it didn't actually add the identity.

Fix it now (run these commands):

Bash
# 1. Make sure the agent is running
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"

# 2. Add the key again (this should prompt for passphrase again)
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

When it asks for the passphrase → just press Enter again (blank).

After that, run:

Bash
ssh-add -l

You should now see a line with ed25519 and your key fingerprint.

Then continue with:

Bash
ssh -T git@github.com

Let me know what ssh-add -l and the GitHub test command return.

Alternative Quick Fix (if it keeps failing)

We can remove the passphrase entirely and add the key:

Bash
ssh-keygen -p -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

(Old passphrase: just press Enter) (New passphrase: press Enter twice to set it blank)

Then repeat the ssh-add steps above.

Reply with the output after trying the first block of commands. We're very close.

3.5s
Fast
Executed code

got this

✅ Good progress — the SSH agent is now running (new PID 758493).

Next command — run this now:

Bash
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
  • When it asks for the passphrase → just press Enter (blank).

Then immediately run these two verification commands and paste the output here:

Bash
ssh-add -l
ssh -T git@github.com

Expected successful output:

  • ssh-add -l should show something like: 256 SHA256:... ed25519 (ED25519)
  • ssh -T git@github.com should say: Hi gptix! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.

Once we see that, we’ll update the remote URL and push.

Just run the ssh-add command above and send me the next screenshot/output. You're doing great — we're one or two steps away from fixing the push issue.

3.5s
Fast
Executed code

got this

✅ This is a common and safe GitHub host key rotation issue.

GitHub changed their SSH host key, and your machine still has the old one cached.

Fix it now (run these exact commands):

Bash
# Remove the old GitHub host key
ssh-keygen -f "/home/gt/.ssh/known_hosts" -R github.com

# Re-test the connection
ssh -T git@github.com

After running the above, you should see:

Hi gptix! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.

Then continue with:

Bash
# Update your repo to use SSH (if not already done)
cd ~/local-side   # ← change to your actual repo folder if different
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:gptix/forgrok.git

# Verify
git remote -v

# Finally push
git push origin main

Please run the first block (ssh-keygen -f ...) now and paste the output, especially the result of ssh -T git@github.com.

This is standard and safe — GitHub publicly announces these key changes periodically. Once this is cleared, your push should work.

3.5s
Fast

got this

✅ This is safe and expected.

The fingerprint shown matches GitHub’s current official Ed25519 host key:

SHA256:+DiY3wvvV6TuJJhbpZisF/zLDA0zPMSvHdkr4UvCOqU

What to do right now:

Type yes and press Enter when it asks:

Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])?
